headline: CATTLE HOLDS $254, GRAINS FACE POLLINATION WEEK
subheadline: Live cattle closed at a 52-week high Friday while beans and wheat broke hard into the holiday; this week's crop conditions and weather pattern decide whether grains find a floor.
lead: Friday's close told two different stories from the same market. Live cattle finished at $254.80, up 2.3% on the session and sitting at 93% of its 52-week range, the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout (ongoing since May 19) removing roughly 2% of weekly slaughter capacity and tightening the supply picture enough to lift the front month hard. Meanwhile, beans dropped 24¾ cents to $11.23 nearby, wheat gave back 14 cents to $6.06, and the Corn Belt is heading into pollination week with a cool, wet pattern forecast across most of the Heartland. Those two things are not in conflict: tight packing capacity props cattle while ample soil moisture keeps weather premium out of corn and beans. The question this week is whether the crop-condition numbers Monday afternoon confirm what the forecast is implying, or deliver a surprise.
the_takeaway: Cattle supply is packing-constrained, not production-constrained. That difference matters all week.
teaser: Cattle at a 52-week high, grains breaking into pollination week. Here is what the calendar means.

one_number.unit: Corn Belt precip probability, next 6-10 days
one_number.context: The 6-to-10-day outlook calls for near- or below-normal temperatures east of the Rockies with above-normal precipitation across most of the Heartland. That pattern, heading directly into pollination, is the reason no weather premium is being priced into corn at $4.17 or beans at $11.23. Cool and wet during pollination is not a threat; it is yield insurance. If the pattern flips toward hot and dry in the 11-to-15-day window, that calculus changes fast.

sections.title: GRAINS INTO POLLINATION
sections.body: Corn closed at **$4.17 nearby** and **$4.44 December**, off half a cent and nearly a third of a cent respectively on Friday. Beans nearby dropped **24¾ cents to $11.23**, though November held better at **$11.43**, off less than 5 cents. Wheat gave back **14 cents to $6.06**, its second sharp session in a row. The soybean oil break that started Wednesday, with oil closing Friday at **$69.69** down 1.7%, has been pulling crush complex products lower across the board since mid-week. What matters now is the weather pattern walking directly into pollination: Brownfield's forecast shows cool temperatures and above-normal precipitation likely across most of the Heartland through the 6-to-10-day window. That is not a weather market in the making; that is a yield-support pattern. **Monday's Crop Progress at 3 PM CT is the first real data point of the week.** Corn was at 42% planted as of late May; by now the crop is well established and condition ratings are the number to watch. Below 65% good-to-excellent on corn would be the first signal the cool, wet start left quality issues. Above 70% and the weather premium stays exactly where it is: zero.
sections.catalyst: Cool, wet 6-10 day forecast for Heartland ahead of pollination; soybean oil break dragging crush complex.
sections.bottom_line: No weather premium until the forecast turns. Monday's condition ratings set the tone.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: oil-break gravity held through Friday close, exactly as the week's pattern suggested.

sections.title: CATTLE AT THE HIGH, CONTEXT REQUIRED
sections.body: Live cattle finished Friday at **$254.80**, up 2.3% on the session and at 93% of its 52-week range. That is a strong number. But context is doing a lot of work here. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan lockout, with 1,700 workers off the floor since May 19 and roughly 6,000 head daily of processing capacity removed, is creating a packing-constrained supply picture rather than a production-constrained one. That distinction matters this week: when packing capacity comes back online, the relief valve opens fast. Feeder cattle, meanwhile, closed **Friday at $366.60**, barely changed, off just 0.2%. The feeder/live spread is still telling you feedlots have pricing power on the sell side but are cautious on the buy side. The U.S.-Iran MOU (Memorandum of Understanding pausing the conflict, announced late last week per Frayne Olson of NDSU Extension) is starting to ease input cost pressures in the broader economy, which is constructive for feed margins over time. **Watch for any resolution signal at the Fort Morgan plant; that is the event that resets the live cattle chart faster than any USDA report this week.**
sections.catalyst: Cargill Fort Morgan lockout removing 2% of weekly slaughter; U.S.-Iran MOU easing input cost backdrop.
sections.bottom_line: Packing constraint, not herd shortage, is driving the high. Resolution ends it.

sections.title: ENERGY FIRMS, METALS FALL
sections.body: WTI crude closed Friday at **$76.54**, up 2.7% on the session. The Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, produced a counterintuitive day: crude firmed as the MOU news crystallized and Iraq's announcement that it will maintain its Syria-Baniyas export route regardless of Hormuz status added a structural supply story to the mix. Diesel and input costs for the 2026 crop are more tied to what crude does over the next 60 days than to any single session. Natural gas added 1.3% to **$3.20**, modest, with Permian gas production running well above prior-year pace. Gold fell hard at **$4,173**, down 3.8%, and silver dropped 6.3% to **$64.91**. Both metals have been breaking since mid-week; when the dollar firms and diplomatic progress reduces the safe-haven bid, precious metals give back fast. The Dollar Index closed at **$100.85**, up 0.6%.
sections.catalyst: Iraq-Syria crude route announcement; Iran MOU reducing Hormuz risk premium; dollar firming on diplomacy.
sections.bottom_line: Crude up on supply route news, not demand; metals falling with safe-haven unwind.
sections.vs_yesterday: Metals: three-day slide continues; safe-haven unwind deepened Friday.

sections.title: WEEK AHEAD: THE DATA CALENDAR
sections.body: This is a forecast-dominant week. Monday at 3 PM CT, USDA releases Crop Progress; corn condition ratings are the number, and the cool wet pattern means good ratings are the base case. **If corn good-to-excellent comes in below 65%, that is the first genuine weather-premium trigger of the season.** Thursday at 7:30 AM CT, weekly export sales will tell you whether China's $17 billion annual US ag purchase commitment (announced May 18, spanning beans, beef, and poultry) is translating into actual buy orders or staying on paper. Soybeans under 300K MT on Thursday keeps the chart in charge. Lean hogs at **$95.03** and Class III Milk at **$16.07** are drifting without a fresh catalyst; neither is likely to move this week without a processing or demand shock. The S&P closed at **$7,500.58**, up 1.1%, which keeps the macro backdrop constructive for risk assets including ag commodities. No clean macro headwinds to start the week.
sections.catalyst: Monday Crop Progress, Thursday export sales, ongoing China purchase commitment tracking.
sections.bottom_line: Crop Progress Monday and exports Thursday are the two events that can move the week.

outside_the_pit.title: Oregon Considers Ballot Measure Criminalizing Ranching and Hunting
outside_the_pit.body: Oregon voters may weigh in on a measure that would criminalize raising livestock for food, hunting, and basic animal husbandry. The Oregon Cattlemen's Association is pushing back hard. This is a state-level story today, but measures like this have a way of migrating; producers nationally should be watching the signature-gathering timeline.

outside_the_pit.title: U.S.-Iran MOU Pauses Conflict, Ag Input Costs Starting to Shift
outside_the_pit.body: NDSU's Frayne Olson flagged this week that the MOU between the U.S. and Iran is beginning to ripple through ag input costs, with energy and fertilizer pricing moving off their Hormuz-premium highs. Frustration remains among producers who locked in high input costs before the diplomatic break; the benefit flows to 2027 budgets more than 2026.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm Still Active on Both Sides of the Border
outside_the_pit.body: Texas and Mexican ranchers are both reporting active New World screwworm pressure, with the situation unresolved at the border despite USDA's ongoing response. Any northward spread into US feeder operations this summer would be a livestock health event with real slaughter-weight and timing implications. Watch the USDA APHIS update cadence this week.

the_more_you_know.title: Pollination week and corn: why the next 10 days matter more than any WASDE
the_more_you_know.body: Corn at $4.17 is sitting 43% off its 52-week low with no weather premium priced in. That reflects a specific bet: that the cool, wet pattern forecast for the Heartland carries through the pollination window, roughly the last week of June into the first week of July for the core Belt. Corn pollination is the one irreversible event in the crop calendar. A plant that runs out of pollen receptivity during a heat or drought stress cannot recover yield the way it can recover from early-season flooding. A single week of 95-degree temperatures with no rainfall during silk emergence can take 20 to 30 bushels per acre off a field that looked fine in June. The reason no weather premium exists today is that the current forecast does not show that scenario. But the 11-to-15-day models are where that can change. Watch the extended forecasts starting Wednesday. If the pattern flips to hot and dry in the July 1 to 5 window, corn at $4.17 will not stay there.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn good-to-excellent below 65% is the first weather-premium trigger of the season; above 70% confirms no-premium thesis holds.
USDA Weekly Export Sales: soybeans under 300K MT means the chart controls the bean story for another week; above 500K MT would be the first real demand signal since the China commitment announcement.
Extended forecast models (11-to-15-day): a pattern flip to hot and dry in the July 1-5 window during pollination is the single highest-impact weather risk. Watch Wednesday's model updates.
Cargill Fort Morgan lockout status: any resolution announcement removes the packing-constraint premium from live cattle faster than any USDA report. No resolution announced as of Friday's close.

Beef Magazine live stream: agriculture meteorologist on El Nino and summer-into-fall outlook. The Intermountain West drought plus El Nino monsoon forecast has direct implications for hay and range conditions.

daily_quote.text: After the rain comes the sun.

source_summary: USDA NASS Crop Progress, CME Group settlement prices, Brownfield Ag News weather outlook, NDSU Extension (Frayne Olson), EIA Short Term Energy Outlook, OilPrice.com, The Fence Post, Beef Magazine, Farm Policy News Illinois.
